steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures being connected
steps.content: The sentence has an independent clause: “NATO's principle of collective defense confers both benefits and obligations.” This must connect to what follows: “the organization's many members… are all bound to defend Canada, the reverse is also true.” The core task is choosing the right punctuation and connector to join them.

steps.title: Match the relationship and apply the rule
steps.content:
Choice A (“obligations: while the”) is the keyed punctuation: the colon introduces the elaboration that follows, and “while the…” begins the explanatory clause.

Choice B uses a semicolon without the connector, choice C uses a comma but is not the keyed answer here, and choice D ends the sentence prematurely.

That matches choice A — A is correct.
